request.question: Hail Flutie was a call made at the game with which managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network calling the action?

title: Norm Clarke
Jonathan Landman
A. Charles Muller
Hail Flutie
Martin Gottlieb
China Economic Information Network
Richard Stolley
Bill Adee
Doug Flutie
Brent Musburger
sentences: Norm Clarke (born 1942 in Terry, Montana) is an American gossip columnist in Las Vegas, Nevada.
He wrote the column "Vegas Confidential" for the "Las Vegas Review-Journal" from 1999 to 2016.
He currently writes the column "Vegas Diary" for the website of the Vegas Stats & Information Network.
His column covers celebrities and near-celebrities and their doings in the bright lights of "Sin City."
Jonathan I. Landman is an American journalist and deputy managing editor at "The New York Times".
Landman became deputy managing editor responsible for digital journalism for "The Times" in August 2005.
He had become assistant managing editor and member of "The Times" masthead in May 2005.
Prior to that, Landman had been acting culture editor from 2004 to 2005.
Previously, he was the assistant managing editor for enterprise from 2003 to 2004.
Landman had been metropolitan editor of "The Times" since 1999 and editor of "The Times"<nowiki>'</nowiki> Week in Review section since 1994.
Before that, he served as deputy editor of the Washington bureau since 1992; assistant editor in Washington from 1991; assistant metropolitan editor from 1990 and assistant national editor from 1989.
Landman joined "The Times" as a copy editor in 1987.
A. Charles Muller (born September 19, 1953) is an academic specializing in Korean Buddhism and East Asian Yogacara, having published numerous books and articles on these topics.
He is a resident of Japan, currently teaching at the University of Tokyo.

He is one of the earliest and most prolific developers of online research resources for the field of Buddhist Studies, being the founder and managing editor of the online Digital Dictionary of Buddhism, the CJKV-English Dictionary, and the H-Buddhism Scholars Information Network, along with having digitized and published numerous reference works.
The Hail Flutie game is a college football game that took place between the Boston College Eagles and the University of Miami Hurricanes on November 23, 1984.
It has been regarded by FOX Sports writer Kevin Hench as among the most memorable moments in sports.
The game is most notable for a last-second Hail Mary pass from quarterback Doug Flutie to wide receiver Gerard Phelan to give Boston College the win.
Miami was the defending national champion and entered the game ranked 12th in the nation.
Boston College was ranked 10th with a record of 8–2 and had already accepted an invitation to the Cotton Bowl Classic at the end of the season.
The game was played at the Miami Orange Bowl, and televised nationally by CBS, with Brent Musburger, Ara Parseghian, and Pat Haden commentating.
Martin Gottlieb is Assistant Managing Editor/Investigations of "Newsday".
From 2012 to 2016, he was Editor of "The Record" of Bergen County, New Jersey.
Previously, from 2008 to 2011, he was Global Editions Editor of the "New York Times"; in this capacity, he oversaw production of the "International Herald Tribune".
Gottlieb also served as Associate Managing Editor, Deputy Culture Editor and National Project Editor of the "Times".
Gottlieb was also Managing Editor of the "New York Daily News" and Editor-in-Chief of "The Village Voice".
China Economic Information Network (中国经济信息网) is a national information network that provides information and analyses of Chinese national macroeconomic trends.
It is sponsored by the State Information Network.
Richard Brockway Stolley (born October 3, 1928) is an American journalist and magazine editor.
He's most well known for his work at "Time" magazine, which he joined in 1953.
He subsequently held a number of roles at the magazine including report, writer, bureau chief, senior editor and managing editor.

In addition to his work at "Time", he has also been Assistant Managing Editor and Managing Editor of "Life" magazine, the founding Managing Editor of "People" magazine and Director of Special Projects for Time Inc
Bill Adee is Chief Operating Officer for Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN) after joining the network in March 2017.
Adee was formerly executive vice president of digital development and operations for the "Chicago Tribune".
He joined the news organization as its sports editor in the spring of 2002.

He previously worked for the "Chicago Sun-Times", "Los Angeles Daily News", "Akron Beacon Journal" of Akron, Ohio, "The Virginian-Pilot" of Norfolk, Virginia, "The Daily Herald' in Arlington Heights, Illinois and the "News Sun" of Waukegan, Illinois.
Douglas Richard Flutie (born October 23, 1962) is a former quarterback in the National Football League (NFL), Canadian Football League (CFL), and United States Football League (USFL).
He first rose to prominence during his college football career at Boston College, where he received the Heisman Trophy and the Davey O'Brien National Quarterback Award in 1984.
His "Hail Flutie" touchdown pass in a game against Miami on November 23, 1984 (dubbed "The Pass") is considered among the greatest moments in college football and American sports history.
Flutie was selected as the 285th pick in the 11th round of the 1985 NFL Draft by the Los Angeles Rams, making him the lowest drafted Heisman Award winner among those who were drafted.
Flutie played that year for the New Jersey Generals of the upstart USFL, having already signed a five-year $5 million contract with them prior to being drafted by the Rams.
In 1986, he signed with the NFL's Chicago Bears, and later played for the New England Patriots, becoming their starting quarterback in 1988.
Brent Woody Musburger ( ; born May 26, 1939) is an American sportscaster, lead broadcaster and managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN).
He retired in January 2017 from the ESPN and ABC television networks.

Formerly with CBS Sports and one of the original members of their program "The NFL Today", Musburger has also covered the NBA, MLB, NCAA football and basketball, and NASCAR and served as a studio host for games, a play-by-play man, and halftime host.
He has also performed postgame wrap-up segments and covered championship trophy presentations.
He is a member of the Montana Broadcaster's Association Hall of Fame.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Hail Flutie was a call made at the game with which managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network calling the action?
hop1_docs: Vegas Stats & Information Network | VSiN is a national sports gambling news network based in Las Vegas, Nevada.  VSiN’s lead on-air personality is hall of fame broadcaster Brent Musburger, who anchors the weekday program called “My Guys in the Desert," which refers to Musburger’s veiled gambling references during his play-by-play of sports contests in earlier parts of his career.  Musburger left ESPN in January 2017 and joined VSiN.

Brent Musburger | Brent Woody Musburger ( ; born May 26, 1939) is an American sportscaster, lead broadcaster and managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN).  He retired in January 2017 from the ESPN and ABC television networks.  Formerly with CBS Sports and one of the original members of their program "The NFL Today", Musburger has also covered the NBA, MLB, NCAA football and basketball, and NASCAR and served as a studio host for games, a play-by-play man, and halftime host.  He has also performed postgame wrap-up segments and covered championship trophy presentations.  He is a member of the Montana Broadcaster's Association Hall of Fame.

Flutie effect | The Flutie effect or Flutie factor refers to the American phenomenon of having a successful college sports team increase the exposure and prominence of a university.  This is named after Boston College's Doug Flutie, whose successful Hail Mary pass in the 1984 game against the University of Miami clinched a win which allegedly played a large role in the increase in applications to Boston College the following year.

Hail Flutie | The Hail Flutie game is a college football game that took place between the Boston College Eagles and the University of Miami Hurricanes on November 23, 1984.  It has been regarded by FOX Sports writer Kevin Hench as among the most memorable moments in sports.  The game is most notable for a last-second Hail Mary pass from quarterback Doug Flutie to wide receiver Gerard Phelan to give Boston College the win.  Miami was the defending national champion and entered the game ranked 12th in the nation.  Boston College was ranked 10th with a record of 8–2 and had already accepted an invitation to the Cotton Bowl Classic at the end of the season.  The game was played at the Miami Orange Bowl, and televised nationally by CBS, with Brent Musburger, Ara Parseghian, and Pat Haden commentating.

Stat (system call) | stat() is a Unix system call that returns file attributes about an inode.  The semantics of stat() vary between operating systems.  As an example, Unix command ls uses this system call to retrieve information on files that includes:

Norm Clarke | Norm Clarke (born 1942 in Terry, Montana) is an American gossip columnist in Las Vegas, Nevada.  He wrote the column "Vegas Confidential" for the "Las Vegas Review-Journal" from 1999 to 2016.  He currently writes the column "Vegas Diary" for the website of the Vegas Stats & Information Network.  His column covers celebrities and near-celebrities and their doings in the bright lights of "Sin City."

Bill Adee | Bill Adee is Chief Operating Officer for Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN) after joining the network in March 2017.  Adee was formerly executive vice president of digital development and operations for the "Chicago Tribune".  He joined the news organization as its sports editor in the spring of 2002.  He previously worked for the "Chicago Sun-Times", "Los Angeles Daily News", "Akron Beacon Journal" of Akron, Ohio, "The Virginian-Pilot" of Norfolk, Virginia, "The Daily Herald' in Arlington Heights, Illinois and the "News Sun" of Waukegan, Illinois.
response.summary_1: Passage 4: the Hail Flutie game "took place ... on November 23, 1984" and "was televised nationally by CBS, with Brent Musburger, Ara Parseghian, and Pat Haden commentating." Passage 2: "Brent Musburger ... is ... managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN)." Bridge: the entity Brent Musburger connects the game's commentators to the VSiN managing editor title; no additional identity link appears missing. Use these quoted phrases (especially "Brent Musburger" + the quoted commentator list) to confirm that the managing editor who called the action is the same Brent Musburger named in the VSiN entry.

VSiN’s lead on-air personality is hall of fame broadcaster Brent Musburger, who anchors the weekday program called “My Guys in the Desert," which refers to Musburger’s veiled gambling references during his play-by-play of sports contests in earlier parts of his career. 1974 WTA Tour | The 1974 WTA Tour was composed of the fourth annual Virginia Slims Circuit and Women’s International Grand Prix, a tour of tennis tournaments for female tennis players, sponsored by Virginia Slims cigarettes.  The WTA signed their first television broadcast contract in 1974, with the broadcasting network CBS with Brent Musburger announcing.
response.summary_2: The Hail Flutie call was made by Brent Musburger. Brent Musburger is the managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN) (see passage 2).
